The rebellion reflected colonial resentment against the policies of deposed King James II.", "Glorious Revolution | The Glorious Revolution, also called the Revolution of 1688, was the overthrow of King James II of England (James VII of Scotland) by a union of English Parliamentarians with the Dutch stadtholder William III, Prince of Orange. William's successful invasion of England with a Dutch fleet and army led to his ascension to the throne as William III of England jointly with his wife, Mary II, James's daughter, after the Declaration of Right, leading to the Bill of Rights 1689.", "Francis Nicholson | Francis Nicholson (12 November 1655 – March 16, 1728 [O.S. March 5, 1727] ) was a British military officer and colonial administrator. His military service included time in Africa and Europe, after which he was sent to North America as leader of the troops supporting Governor, Sir Edmund Andros in the Dominion of New England. There he distinguished himself, and was appointed lieutenant governor of the Dominion in 1688. After news of the Glorious Revolution and the overthrow of King James II reached the colonies in 1689, Andros was himself overthrown in the Boston Revolt. I can now formulate a complete answer to the question.", "tool_name_1": "finish", "tool_args_1": {}, "observation_1": "Completed."}, "reasoning": "The revolution that led to the overthrow of King James II of England is known as the Glorious Revolution, which occurred in 1688. This event involved a coalition of English Parliamentarians and the Dutch stadtholder William III, who invaded England and ultimately ascended to the throne alongside his wife, Mary II. The news of this revolution reached the American colonies, leading to significant unrest. In particular, it resulted in the overthrow of Sir Edmund Andros, the governor of the Dominion of New England, during the Boston Revolt in 1689. This revolt was fueled by colonial resentment against Andros's administration, which was seen as a continuation of James II's unpopular policies.", "answer": "The revolution that led to the overthrow of King James II of England is the Glorious Revolution. attributes.mlflow.spanOutputs: ["Leisler's Rebellion | Leisler's Rebellion was an uprising in late 17th century colonial New York in which German American merchant and militia captain Jacob Leisler seized control of the colony's south and ruled it from 1689 to 1691. The uprising took place in the aftermath of Britain's Glorious Revolution and the 1689 Boston revolt in the Dominion of New England, which had included New York. The rebellion reflected colonial resentment against the policies of deposed King James II.", "Glorious Revolution | The Glorious Revolution, also called the Revolution of 1688, was the overthrow of King James II of England (James VII of Scotland) by a union of English Parliamentarians with the Dutch stadtholder William III, Prince of Orange. William's successful invasion of England with a Dutch fleet and army led to his ascension to the throne as William III of England jointly with his wife, Mary II, James's daughter, after the Declaration of Right, leading to the Bill of Rights 1689.", "Francis Nicholson | Francis Nicholson (12 November 1655 – March 16, 1728 [O.S. March 5, 1727] ) was a British military officer and colonial administrator. His military service included time in Africa and Europe, after which he was sent to North America as leader of the troops supporting Governor, Sir Edmund Andros in the Dominion of New England. There he distinguished himself, and was appointed lieutenant governor of the Dominion in 1688. After news of the Glorious Revolution and the overthrow of King James II reached the colonies in 1689, Andros was himself overthrown in the Boston Revolt. Nicholson himself was soon caught up in the civil unrest from Leisler's Rebellion in New York Town, and afterwards fled to England."]

search_wikipedia

[[ ## observation_0 ## ]]
[1] «Leisler's Rebellion | Leisler's Rebellion was an uprising in late 17th century colonial New York in which German American merchant and militia captain Jacob Leisler seized control of the colony's south and ruled it from 1689 to 1691. The uprising took place in the aftermath of Britain's Glorious Revolution and the 1689 Boston revolt in the Dominion of New England, which had included New York. The rebellion reflected colonial resentment against the policies of deposed King James II.»
[2] «Glorious Revolution | The Glorious Revolution, also called the Revolution of 1688, was the overthrow of King James II of England (James VII of Scotland) by a union of English Parliamentarians with the Dutch stadtholder William III, Prince of Orange. William's successful invasion of England with a Dutch fleet and army led to his ascension to the throne as William III of England jointly with his wife, Mary II, James's daughter, after the Declaration of Right, leading to the Bill of Rights 1689.»
[3] «Francis Nicholson | Francis Nicholson (12 November 1655 – March 16, 1728 [O.S. March 5, 1727] ) was a British military officer and colonial administrator. His military service included time in Africa and Europe, after which he was sent to North America as leader of the troops supporting Governor, Sir Edmund Andros in the Dominion of New England. There he distinguished himself, and was appointed lieutenant governor of the Dominion in 1688. After news of the Glorious Revolution and the overthrow of King James II reached the colonies in 1689, Andros was himself overthrown in the Boston Revolt. Nicholson himself was soon caught up in the civil unrest from Leisler's Rebellion in New York Town, and afterwards fled to England.»"}
